data.name: Jordan Pickford
data.description: Likes to make himself heard. James Tarkowski says his Everton teammate is “top-end” but “crackers … me and him fall out constantly. He shouts my name constantly from behind me so I'm constantly looking at him and he's just barking at me to bark at someone else. And I can never really understand what he's saying because he's 40 yards away, shouting, screaming at me. I'm like: 'Will you just come and tell me what you want …’” Last year he broke the record for consecutive England clean sheets. He would have been called Jordan Pigford but his dad, Lee, changed the family name because he was tired of being called Piggy at school. Pickford says that if he wasn’t a footballer, he’d be in motocross. “I grew up watching it. We would stay in motorhomes and caravans at the racetrack as kids and it was mint. It gives me proper adrenaline, and it is one of the things I love.”

data.name: Ezri Konsa
data.description: Signed for Charlton at 11 after starting out at Senrab FC, the Sunday League club in London’s Forest Gate which also produced, among others, Jermain Defoe, Sol Campbell and John Terry. Konsa has the word “blessed” tattooed on his left hand. “I got it in my second season at Charlton,” he said in 2021. “It reminds me how far I’ve come and how blessed I am. It was difficult growing up where I grew up. There was a lot of crime and violence, but I am lucky to have an older brother who kept me out of trouble and kept me grounded. It was tough, but me and my family and friends dealt with it and we got out of it. Hopefully what I’m doing now can inspire the next generation.” He signed for Villa from Brentford in 2019 for £12m, and made his England debut against Brazil in 2024. Has royal backing from Villa fan Prince William. Konsa: “He called me a Rolls-Royce once. That got stuck in my head. I’ll take that all day. It was really nice.”

data.name: Nico O'Reilly
data.description: A City academy graduate having signed aged eight, O’Reilly’s rise has been rapid. A full debut in 2024, a first England call-up a year later, and now a World Cup. “It’s mad, I never thought I’d be in this position so soon,” he said last year, insisting his breakthrough hadn’t changed him. “I've still got the same friends, I still do the same stuff, I still go to Tesco with my mum.” He says his mother, Holli, is his inspiration, and she recorded a surprise video message for him when he signed his new City contract last September, saying: “I always remember, when you were about three months old, I looked at you and I knew you were special. I said to Nana: 'He is special,' and she laughed at me like she does, and says: 'Everyone thinks their kids are special.' But son, I knew you were, and always believed in you, and always will. Don’t ever change. You’re absolutely smashing life, and I love you.”

data.name: Declan Rice
data.description: The most expensive British player ever at £105m, just ahead of the £100m Jack Grealish. Rice was released from Chelsea’s academy aged 14 and joined West Ham after a trial. He stayed there until 2023 having become one of only three Hammers captains, with Billy Bonds and Bobby Moore, to lead the club to a major trophy. Now a Premier League champion with Arsenal, he made three appearances for Ireland before committing to England in 2019. Also loves golf, and did some modelling at London Fashion Week in 2024, wearing a navy jacket with white lace-up tie. “You know what, it was so good,” he told Sky. “I love fashion, this is really special.” Has talked previously about developing his own look, after growing up with “mum always telling me how to dress … I’m always looking for a pair of trousers that sit above my shoes well. Not too low, not too high. That’s a key thing for me, how a pair of trousers sit on shoes.”

data.name: John Stones
data.description: Made his England debut 12 years ago, and has won everything at club level. “I never thought I'd be a professional, let alone playing for England, playing in the Premier League, never mind winning the Premier League,” he said in 2023. A Barnsley academy graduate, known as the Barnsley Beckenbauer, he’s always been keen not to be seen as too cultured on the ball. “Of course, I’ll kick it into row Z if necessary,” he said a decade ago after his £47.5m move from Everton. “I do the dirty stuff that every defender should do and should be good at. The rest of it is a bonus, where I can play out and start attacks.” He’s popular among teammates; last year his City club-mate Bernardo Silva revealed why he named his dog after Stones. “I was thinking of getting a dog and I said: ‘John, one day if I get a dog, I will name it after you. Would you be happy about it?’ He said: ‘No, you can’t.' But then I just did it. I like the name John. John Silva, I think it’s a good name.”

data.name: Marc Guéhi
data.description: The second Côte d'Ivoire-born England player after his former Palace teammate Wilfried Zaha, who ultimately opted for his homeland. Guéhi left Chelsea for Palace, via a loan at Swansea, in 2021, and won the FA Cup there. Son of a church minister, he called last summer’s collapsed move to Liverpool and subsequent tranger to City in January “God’s plan [coming] to full fruition”. Known for being calm and thoughtful, but he has his eye on a different future when football’s over. “I'd love to be a WWE wrestler,” he told the BBC. “Growing up, watching WWE … It can be dangerous but the performance side of it … I'm a good waffler on the mic … making people believe you're the bad guy or the good guy.” Spends his spare time reading or in the cinema “because no one goes to the cinema now, so it's nice to be in a cinema on your own”, and has plans to resume drumming. “I drummed at church for a long time. I drummed at school. I don't have a drum set but I really want one. That's a really good way to enjoy music and escape.”

data.name: Bukayo Saka
data.description: Feels like a long-term fixture in England squads, having made his debut six years ago, but is still only 24. His first name, chosen by his Nigerian grandmother, means “adds to happiness”; his nickname, Little Chilli, was given to him by former teammate Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang due to his spicy shooting in training. “He just kept calling me it and then it became a thing, and now it's a big thing.” In 2024 Nandos brought out an official peri-peri Saka sauce, with Saka saying: “I’ve been going to Nando’s since I was little so to have my own peri-peri sauce is quite mad.” During three months out with a torn hamstring last year he bought a new puppy, Tucker the cockapoo, and got stuck into reading, with one book in particular, Eckhart Tolle’s The Power of Now: A Guide to Spiritual Enlightenment, striking a chord. “Sometimes I’d think: ‘Oh, am I going to come back in the best shape?’ Or, in the past: ‘What could I have done to prevent injury?’ But all that is not necessary. One of the best things I took from the book is to always ask yourself: 'What’s necessary in this moment right now?' And try to live that way.”

data.name: Elliot Anderson
data.description: The Whitley Bay-born Anderson was named in the senior Scotland squad for the first time in August 2023, eligible to represent them through his grandmother. But he changed his mind soon after. “I left camp after a few days and decided I just needed more time,” he said after being called into the senior England squad last year. “I didn’t want to dive into anything, so I’m really thankful I’ve done that now. I’m definitely English. It’s just I hadn’t really been called up by England through the age levels when I was younger so I thought I’d take the opportunity. But once I got that belief that I was good enough to play for England, it hit me.” Newcastle sold him to Forest in 2024 to meet Premier League profitability and sustainability rules. Anderson is a graduate of the famous Wallsend Boys Club, like Alan Shearer, Peter Beardsley and Michael Carrick. His older brother, Wil, appeared on the 11th season of Love Island, entering the villa on day 12, and being dumped on day 37.

data.name: Harry Kane
data.description: Overtook Pelé’s international scoring record last year, but it wasn’t until last summer that he finally lifted a club trophy, when Bayern won their 34th Bundesliga. “It was a weight on me,” he said. “As the years go by you start to think: ‘Is it going to happen?’” It was a rough road to the top: at 19 he was loaned to Norwich, missed a sitter on his debut, played five times, scored no goals. He also had a hard time on loan at Leicester – which partly motivated his creation of the Harry Kane Foundation, supporting mental health charities. “There wasn’t just a straight line to the top. I had to really go through some tough times. I’m learning more and more [about mental health] and for sure it’s something that I’ll really try to take on and help as much as I can when my career has finished.” In his spare time he plays golf, collects watches and plays chess, inspired by watching The Queen's Gambit. He told GQ in 2024: “Since that came out I've started to learn and I'm about 1200 rated on the 10-minute version on Chess.com. It's fairly decent.”

data.name: Jude Bellingham
data.description: Raised in Stourbridge, Bellingham used to turn up at Birmingham for training aged 14 in his burgundy Priory school uniform. The striker Lukas Jutkiewicz said: “We’re doing finishing drills and this kid’s joining in with the first team, he’s faking to shoot, putting senior pros on their arses, chopping and then rolling it into the other corner. I thought to myself – and I know I wasn’t the only one – ‘Just who is this?’” Bellingham graduated from Loughborough College with a BTec Level 3 Certificate in Sport in 2021, completing it remotely after moving to Borussia Dortmund. Commercial deals include Louis Vuitton, Lucozade, his own JB Adidas signature collection, his own app, and, in 2024, modelling men’s underpants for Kim Kardashian’s Skims brand. He had an uneasy moment with Thomas Tuchel last year when the England manager told TalkSport about Bellingham’s “edge” during games which “can be a bit repulsive”. Tuchel clarified later: “I used this word unintentionally. There was no message, no hidden agenda. I created the headlines and I’m sorry for the upset. I should have known better.”

data.name: Marcus Rashford
data.description: An MBE-holder, author and poverty campaigner who took on and beat Boris Johnson twice. Rashford arrived at the World Cup on a high after a restorative season at Barcelona. Last October he pointed to the high turnover of managers at Manchester United as a reason for his inconsistency there: “I feel like I’ve been in an inconsistent environment for a long time … In order to be consistent in anything, not just sport, you need consistent variables in your life. We’ve just had so many changes.” He was raised in Wythenshawe, the youngest of five, with his mum, Mel, working three jobs to keep the family going. His experiences informed his off-field work since. He co-wrote The Breakfast Club Adventures book series, and the self-help bestseller for children You Are a Champion. It starts with this dedication: “To every young person who is trying to find their way. To my mum, for helping me believe that dreams can come true.”

data.name: Trevoh Chalobah
data.description: Missed out on Thomas Tuchel’s initial squad but was called up after Tino Livramento was ruled out on the eve of England’s first game against Croatia. Born in Sierra Leone, Chalobah joined Chelsea aged eight and developed via loans at Ipswich, Huddersfield, Lorient and Crystal Palace. If he wasn’t a footballer he says he would be a table tennis player, but fashion is a bigger interest. “Fashion has been a passion of mine since I was young,” he said in 2021. “Obviously you see stuff on TV and find a lot of fashion artists online. When I was in France, I did a cover shoot with an upcoming Italian brand called Valore. I really enjoy doing things like that.” He also enjoys playing Uno with Ezri Konsa and using his laptop to “YouTube a lot of sermons, a lot of Premier League highlights, a lot of football highlights and a lot of cookery stuff as well. I like to cook and learn things.” His brother, Nathaniel, now at Sheffield Wednesday, played six minutes and 54 seconds as a sub for England against Spain in 2018 for his only cap – the second shortest England career after Martin Kelly’s two minutes and 39 seconds.

data.name: Dean Henderson
data.description: Henderson left Manchester United to join Palace in 2023 after an eye-catching loan at Forest, and his reputation keeps growing. Highlights so far include saving Omar Marmoush’s penalty at Wembley to help Palace win their first FA Cup, which he later credited to “my magic cap … when I put it on I knew we had a chance”. He’s often seen leading Palace fans’ Shakira tribute song about that Cup victory during and after games. He says he loves going to England training camps because “sometimes day one is a special night and we get brownies and custard. That’s always something to look forward to. A day before a game is rice pudding for dessert. The rice pudding here is sensational, it’s got little white chocolate chips in it. It’s something I always look forward to.”

data.name: Jordan Henderson
data.description: A vocal leader, fresh from a convincing comeback season in English football with Brentford. Was heavily criticised in 2023 after leaving Liverpool for the Saudi Pro League club Al-Ettifaq, given his previous support for LGBTQ+ rights, and was booed by fans during an England game soon afterwards. He ended his Saudi contract after 24 weeks and moved to Ajax, but was left out of the Euro 2024 squad. When England made the final, he called a van-hire company and made the seven-hour journey to Berlin with his family to watch, surprising England fans at service stations along the way. “When we needed the toilet or something, or popping in the garages, it was quite funny to see some of the fans,” he says. “Actually, it was quite nice to be with them. But obviously I would have rather been with the team.” He earned an international recall last March. Was awarded an MBE in 2021 for helping drive the #PlayersTogether NHS fundraising campaign during the Covid pandemic.

data.name: Dan Burn
data.description: Released by Newcastle aged 11 just before Christmas in 2003. Burn said it was “devastating” but “I was pretty crap, if I’m honest”. By 13 he was down to nine fingers after a fence-climbing accident, and at 16 he was working at Asda in Blyth pushing trolleys. “Normally the lads who are getting picked up to play in the youth teams are getting picked up around 15, 16. I didn’t end up signing for anyone. I went to sixth form and was pushing trolleys. If you had said then that I would be in the Premier League, I’d have laughed at you.” But he kept going in non-league and Darlington signed him at 17, starting a journey which, in 2022, featured a £13m return to Newcastle from Brighton. When he scored in the Carabao Cup final and won his first England call-up in 2025, the Asda in Blyth framed a “Burn 33” high-vis trolley-pusher jacket in tribute. Store manager Dean: “We’re hoping that Dan will pay us a visit soon, along with the trophy.”

data.name: Kobbie Mainoo
data.description: When Mainoo became the first English teenager to score in an FA Cup final for 43 years in 2024, Paul Scholes wrote on Instagram: “Read a few comparisons between me and this kid. Don’t waste your time, he is 10 times the player I was at 19.” His progress at United was smooth until he came up against Ruben Amorim’s 3-4-2-1. His half-brother, the former Love Island star Jordan Mainoo-Hames, wore a “free Kobbie Mainoo” T-shirt during a 4-4 draw with Bournemouth, but regular football didn’t come again until Amorim left. Restored by Michael Carrick, who had worked with him at youth level, Mainoo proved his worth. “When there's new managers, they have their way they want to play,” Mainoo said in May, reflecting on his time in exile. “If they think you don't fit that, then you don't fit that. I'd say my family and my friends helped me see the light at the end of the tunnel. They knew it would swing back my way at some point. I just had to be patient."

data.name: Morgan Rogers
data.description: Rogers says his loan spells at Lincoln, Bournemouth and Blackpool – before his move to Middlesbrough in 2023 – “were everything to me”. He told the Times: “I had to go through a lot of struggles, a lot of pain, a lot of hard days … I had to find myself as a player, and I had to find that out in the lower leagues – what works in a professional game – because what works in [Manchester City] academy football is not the same in League One or the Championship.” He moved to Villa for £8m in 2024. He says he was “a bit of a geek for football knowledge” as a boy: “Learning players, learning teams and stuff. Me and my dad were quite big on it – he’d ask me questions and I’d answer them. I’d be able to name random players across the world and stuff. I’d try and be the cleverest person in the room when it came to football.” He has multiple tattoos, including one on his left forearm listing the birthdays of his parents and three brothers. “That is my favourite one. My most sentimental one.”

data.name: Anthony Gordon
data.description: Arrives at the World Cup on the back of one of the most surprising transfers in recent years as Barcelona paid £60.7m plus add-ons to sign him from Newcastle. He was bullish during his presentation, saying: “There is a burning fire in my belly to win here. [I bring] my intensity, work rate, technical ability. I like to score goals, assist and help the team win games.” It is quite a turnaround for a player who, released by Liverpool aged 11, joined Everton and stayed until 2023 when, via a controversial transfer request, he joined Newcastle for £40m. His senior England debut came against Brazil in 2024. He’s a fan of motivational books, with favourites including Winning: The Unforgiving Race to Greatness by Tim Grover and Never Finished: Unshackle Your Mind and Win the War Within by the retired Navy Seal and ultra-marathon runner David Goggins. During Euro 2024 he memorably turned up to face a regular England press conference covered in cuts having fallen off an electric bike while trying to take a video on his phone, having misunderstood the brakes. “The bike has not just slowed down, it has fully come to a stop … and I haven’t.”

data.name: Ollie Watkins
data.description: Scored the last-minute goal that sent England to the Euro 2024 final. “I don’t think I’ve hit a ball that sweet before.” He said it felt surreal for someone who, a few years earlier, had been struggling to get into Exeter’s side in League Two. “I never imagined it. Obviously you can dream but I’m a realist.” His pre-match routine involves music: “The closer it gets to kick-off, I like to listen to heavier hip-hop, so 50 Cent and his old-school Get Rich or Die Tryin' album or Rick Ross – something like that.” But he chose to sing Never Too Much by Luther Vandross as his initiation song when signing for Villa. Gets his love of music from his mum, Delsi-May, a professional singer, who told the Times in 2021 how she first spotted Ollie’s football potential by the seaside at home in Torquay. “We used to take the kids out, and there was a little machine where you put a 2p in, and a little rubber ball fell out. He’d stand there and you’d drop it, and he’d kick it. He never used to miss. He was only nine months old.”

data.name: Noni Madueke
data.description: Signed by Crystal Palace when he was nine, he switched to Spurs three years later and, after starring at England youth levels, was approached by Manchester United. But he rejected them, opting instead to leave England aged 16 for PSV Eindhoven. “I just wanted to be integrated into a first-team setup as quickly as possible.” He told GQ he learned Dutch quickly because “I didn't want anyone talking shit about me on the field … Like: ‘I know exactly what you're saying!’” He shone there, and Chelsea signed him from PSV for £28.5m in 2023. Arsenal paid £48.5m for him in 2025 – a deal criticised by some Arsenal fans with a #NoToMadueke campaign due to his mixed form, and the fee. “I took it in my stride. You are in the public eye, people can say what they want.” He’s one of the England squad’s fashion fans. “I feel like if you look good, you feel good, you play good. Football, music, fashion, it’s all connected for me. It’s how I express who I am, and each one feeds into the other.”

data.name: Eberechi Eze
data.description: Last summer Eze was flying: an FA Cup winner with Crystal Palace, an emerging England regular, and a new £60m signing for boyhood club Arsenal. But – apart from a memorable derby hat-trick against Spurs, and two in the return fixture – he has struggled to make a regular impact in a title-winning season at the Emirates. Still, he has plenty of resilience to draw on, having been through the trauma of being released by multiple clubs, including Arsenal, as a youngster. Looking back he said he coped thanks to “my trust and faith in God, I just allowed it to fuel me and fill me up, which was important. It doesn't mean that it wasn’t difficult. It's still difficult to get through those times. I remember thinking: ‘Why is this so hard?' But the love for football never, never changed.” He’s also a keen chess player, having been taught to play by his former Palace teammate Michael Olise. Eze won the £15,000 first prize in a Chess.com amateur tournament in May last year, beating the YouTuber Sapnap in the final.

data.name: Ivan Toney
data.description: The big surprise in Thomas Tuchel’s squad – to most people at least, but maybe not to the man himself. Having left Brentford for Al-Ahli in 2024 he told the Guardian the following year that the level of football in the Saudi Pro League was “on a par with the Premier League … If Al-Ahli were in the Premier League, we’d be close to the top four. People shouldn’t turn their noses up.” His experience of playing in the heat could have helped him edge out Dominic Calvert-Lewin, Dominic Solanke and Danny Welbeck for a place in this squad. He says critics should “go and try to play in a sauna and see if they can cope”. He missed the 2022 World Cup due to the investigation into breaches of FA betting rules which led to an eight-month ban. “The biggest punishment for me was missing out on the World Cup,” he said in 2023. “I felt like somebody's out to get me to stop me from playing for England. That was the lowest point.” Famed for his no-look penalty technique, including one during a shootout in Euro 2024. “I never look at the ball,” he said later. “Some people may see it as crazy. But it is just my routine.”

data.name: James Trafford
data.description: Returned to City – where he started aged 12 – from Burnley last year, after keeping 29 clean sheets in 45 Championship games. He grew up on his parents’ farm in Cumbria, and his move to City in 2015 made the local paper, headlined: “James, 12, signs for mega-rich Man City.” His dad told the Times & Star: “He helps us get the sheep into the field – it's one of his favourite jobs. But I've never said that he must farm. You've got to let them go their own way … we're extremely proud of him.” James Jr told the paper: “I'm not nervous. The first-team players are in the same place we train. I've met Gaël Clichy and Vincent Kompany, and I've seen Joe Hart training. It's really exciting." He still keeps his hand in whenever he goes home to the farm. “When I was with dad last time I was bedding up all the sheep and putting hay out for the ones outside. We’ve always done that.”

data.name: Reece James
data.description: A Chelsea academy graduate, James started out as a striker because “I idolised Didier Drogba … He played in my team, he played in my position and I always looked up to him. I just wanted to be like him, score like him and celebrate like him.” But his coaches saw it differently, moving him first to midfield, then, aged 15, to right-back. “I hated it for two or three years. But then one day it just clicked.” He and Lauren are the first brother and sister to play senior England football in the modern era. They give each other space, though. Lauren said in 2022: “Anything to do with football, we both know what we need to do so we just leave the other one to it. But outside of football, we have a great relationship. I think with our age gap that helps, with it only being two years … He is a funny guy.” He overcame three difficult seasons of injury and fitness problems to be ready for this summer.

data.name: Djed Spence
data.description: Spence says he keeps a mental note of all his doubters. He was sent on loan three times by Spurs before establishing himself, and was also loaned out when he was at Middlesbrough in 2021, with the then manager, Neil Warnock, warning him he would be in non-league football in five years unless he changed his attitude. After winning promotion with Forest, whom he joined on loan from Boro, he tweeted a photo of himself with the trophy, smoking a cigar, and tagged Warnock. “I wouldn’t say I particularly have a list but I do have a mental note of people who doubted me, for sure, and it does feel good to prove them wrong,” he says. “I’ve always had that from a young age, I’ve always had to fight for my position.” When he made his England debut last year he became the first Muslim to do so for the senior men’s side. “It’s a blessing, it’s amazing. I was surprised, really, the first ever. If I can do it, you can do it. It’s [important] not just for Muslim kids but for any child of any faith. Put your mind to something and you can do it, man.”

data.name: Jarell Quansah
data.description: Joined Liverpool aged five, captained them at youth level, and broke into the first team in 2023. But two years later, after 58 appearances, he joined Leverkusen for £30m and scored five minutes into his Bundesliga debut. He said in March that leaving for Germany had been a “no-brainer … I just wanted to play. I felt like I could play at the top level, the Bundesliga's a top league. I think you just have maybe a gut feeling. I took time to myself to reflect on everything that was going on at Liverpool, everything I thought I could've done better and what was best for my career really, that was it. When the Leverkusen guys came in and I saw everything that they wanted – I think, from that moment, I knew it was the right decision." He won his first England cap last year against Albania, calling it “one of the greatest days of my life”. His brother, Keenan, plays for Slough Town.
